text:
An Olympian at theParis Gameshas been left fuming after allegedly falling ill following a swim in the River Seine.

The build-up to the Olympics was dominated by issues surrounding the River Seine, with complaints about the levels of bacteria in its water.

Reportssuggest French officials spent over £1.2billion in an attempt to clean the river, which was due to host several of the Games’ events.

Early efforts were rendered useless though, with testing in June revealing the E.coli levels were 10 times higher than the permitted amount.

It caused disruption to events as well, with thetriathlon having been delayedafter heavy rainfall prompted fresh concerns about the water quality in the Seine.

The events went ahead as planned, if not a little later than scheduled, but the fallout continues to dominate headlines ahead of Sunday’s closing ceremony.

Taking to social media to share her frustrations, Leonie Beck said: “Vommitted [sic] nine times yesterday plus diarrhoea. Water quality in the Seine approved.”

The German athlete signed off her complaint with a sarcastic green tick emoji.





Beck isn’t the only Olympian to have been left feeling poorly after swimming the Parisian river, after Belgian triathlete Claire Michel was taken to hospital.

It forced the Belgian team to withdraw from the mixed triathlon event, as they didn’t have a replacement available.

It sparked a furious response from team-mate Jolien Vermeylen, who dubbed the decision to use the Seine as “bulls**t.”

The 30-year-old, who finished 24th in the women’s individual event, told Flemish TV channel VTM: “The Seine has been dirty for a hundred years, so they can’t say that the safety of the athletes is a priority. That’s bulls**t!

Triathletes remain tight-lipped about experience of swimming in Seine. But Belgium's Jolien Vermeylen didn't hold back."I drank a lot of water, so we'll know tomorrow whether I'm ill or not. It doesn't taste like Coca-Cola or Sprite. 1/2

“I took pro-biotics, I drank my Yakult, I couldn’t do more. I had the idea of not drinking water, but yes, it failed.

“It was now or never, and they couldn’t cancel the race completely either. Now they just have to hope that there won’t be too many sick athletes.”

We use your sign-up to provide content in ways you've consented to and to improve our understanding of you. This may include adverts from us and 3rd parties based on our understanding. You can unsubscribe at any time. Read ourPrivacy Policy

Swimming had been banned in the river for over 100 years, but the Paris 2024 organisers insisted on using it for the Games.

The opening ceremony took place on the river, but, no doubt to the delight of the athletes and spectators alike, it’s back to land for Sunday night’s closing ceremony, which will instead take place in the Stade de France.

title: Olympic swimmer fuming after 'vomiting nine times and suffering from diarrhoea' | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Amos Murphy
description: Olympians have blasted the organisers' decision to use the River Seine for swimming events.
